❗ PARTNER ANNOUNCEMENT❗ We are excited to announce our partnership with Cyberwave. Cyberwave Hacks is a student-led hackathon organization that hosts free events, including game jams, where participants can compete for prizes and learn new skills. During this partnership, Cyberwave will be joining us in co-hosting the 2026 STEMEd AI Hackathon (p.s. stay tuned for our posts announcing the event in detail). Reliving the scenes from Gen AI Hack We had 12 teams of builders come together with one clear mission — build a product and ship it. Over 48 hours, ideas moved beyond discussion and into deployment. Mentor hours were intense, GenAI sessions were practical, and final pitches reflected real execution. This is what GenAI Hack stands for — moving from encouragement to action, from concept to live product. Explore all the products launched during the hackathon: genaihack.io/listings In partnership with SparkHub Co-partner: Synetecs #GenAIHack26

We are all in on leveraging AI across the business at RELEX Solutions and I love seeing notes like this from our less technical team members. For the record we were started by a team of data scientists so our focus on automated decision making has a 20 year heritage across every genre of AI through the decades.

Most education tools talk AT students. Alongside 200+ builders at Return of the Agents today, hosted by AI Valley , we hacked and built one that listens, understands context, and talks back WITH them. ElevenLabs transcribes voice. Anthropic 's Claude generates answers with full context. MiniMax Speech-26 speaks it back through multiple voice profiles selected automatically by content type. Technical skill-building shouldn't require anyone to already understand the material before they can ask questions. Thanks AI Valley for hosting a great Hackathon! It was fun building with you Surbhi Bhatnagar !

Hackathon under way! Engineers from around the world just kicked off Nairon's first 24-hour Remote Hackathon! The challenge: Build an AI-powered solution for clinical trial patient matching. People are dying because they don't know trials exist for their condition. Trials are failing because they can't find patients. Massive matching problem. No manual coding allowed. Agents only 🤖 We're evaluating AI nativeness - how well they direct AI tools, not how fast they type. We'll be checking in throughout to see how they think, what questions they ask, and how they iterate. 24 hours. Let's see what they build!

Tired of repeating the same tedious browser tasks every single day? 😩 Yeah… we were too. So we decided to fix it in just 48 hours. We built Ditto: a smart browser agent that watches your screen recording once → then automates the entire workflow on your machine. No more manual repetition. Just record → relax → repeat and run whenever. Yusuke , Kerui and I (currently in arrayah 's accelerator) just hacked this together at the Mistral Worldwide Hackathon in Sydney. Huge shoutout to the Mistral AI , Iterate , and UNSW Founders for putting on an epic event! If you're curious and want to see Ditto in action, just shoot me a DM as we'd love to give you a quick demo.

Judgai AI Hackathon Judge is an autonomous system that evaluates hackathon projects the way a real jury would - by analyzing the GitHub repository, demo video, and project claims together. Problem: - The number of participants in hackathons is growing rapidly. - Hackathon projects are becoming increasingly complex. - Manual evaluation is time-consuming and not always objective. - Judges may overlook important files or details of the demonstration. And in general, project reviews can take months - this can help fix that.

top_visible_comments.comment: How does it solve context window problem? You feed it with year long memories and expect it to follow up on something you discussed 8 months ago. How would that fit into 128k context window (practical limit for most models today)? Even if you had a database solution, how would your conversation hit the 8 months ago memory and follow up on that? In a real conversation, you expect someone to randomly just remember something from past and bring tha topic up, just like how your agent asked about the progress about your project. Since your project comes in the most recent memory and probably within the model context it works. Would you expect it to work for the above scenario? Example - Your friend suddenly remembered discussing about doing a side gig together, a few months ago and asks you if you’re still up for it.

this is such a strong move. building and shipping a deep integration while sponsoring a yc hackathon is elite execution. voice agents without memory always feel shallow, and solving that with almost instant latency and user profiles is a real unlock. love how focused supermemory is becoming on being the infrastructure layer agents actually need. this is the kind of quiet upgrade that makes the whole ecosystem better.
The gap between demo agents and production agents is still massive. Most of the real work is in reliability engineering, not prompt tricks.
Bro you did clean shave na 😂! Just kidding , this is the thing what we needed memory long term in voice ai🫡
Honestly, this hits home. The most frustrating part of voice agents is starting from zero every time

Wild Dhravya Shah ! Love this. Critical technologies including AI is on Australia’s National Innovation Visa priority list which is designed to support top talent to Australia by granting them permanent residency. so I find it fascinating what’s happening around the world when it comes to this space! Well done!
It is the initial handshake between whatever channel and PipeCat to initiate the conversation that takes a long time (2 to 3 secs). The dead space compromise user experience.
This is fantastic! Supercharging voice agents with deep memory is a game-changer for building truly intuitive AI experiences. Excited to see how this evolves!
Dhravya Shah This Voice AI memory for @pipecat_ai with instant latency directly impacts retention and operational gains.

title: jack dorsey just fired 4,000 people and replaced them with AI. block's stock jumped 24%. and honestly? i'm not surprised. here's how i look at it. AI doesn't get tired. it doesn't need three… | Sachi Gupta
headline: jack dorsey just fired 4,000 people and replaced them with AI.
post_text: jack dorsey just fired 4,000 people and replaced them with AI. block's stock jumped 24%. and honestly? i'm not surprised. here's how i look at it. AI doesn't get tired. it doesn't need three follow ups to finish a task. it just does the work. faster. over and over again. that's why headcount is shrinking. not because people aren't talented. but because a huge chunk of what most teams do every day is REPETITIVE. dorsey proved it publicly. others are doing it quietly. we see this every week with the teams we work with at thinklylabs. the ones actually getting ROI from AI have two things figured out 1. adding ai to operational repetitive tasks. 2. designing for multiplication, not replacement. the founders still debating whether AI is overhyped are going to feel this the hard way. faster output. same results. smaller teams. this is just the beginning. before you go chasing the next AI tool, just map out where your team is losing time to work that repeats itself. that's your starting point.

top_visible_comments.comment: Pushing for >15% WoW growth is the only way to find the ceiling—love the focus on Revenue as the single source of truth, Oncel. As a Product Clarity Operator, the biggest risk I see for YC teams hitting these numbers is 'Revenue Noise.' When growth is the only KPI, it’s easy to start building every custom feature request just to keep the 15\% alive. That’s usually how 'Clarity Rot' sets in and kills A-round momentum. Rooting for the Patientdesk team to keep raising the bar!
May your team attain even greater heights. After all, the only way to achieve the impossible is to leave the realm of the possible.

title: Excited to announce I lost the CSI-SPIT hackathon recently 🥳 , and within a week I was shamelessly at Veermata Jijabai Technological Institute (VJTI) for another (hackathon). Fun part is first… | Devansh Raulo | 18 comments
headline: Excited to announce I lost the CSI-SPIT hackathon recently 🥳 , and within a week I was shamelessly at Veermata Jijabai Technological Institute (VJTI) for another (hackathon).
post_text: Excited to announce I lost the CSI-SPIT hackathon recently 🥳 , and within a week I was shamelessly at Veermata Jijabai Technological Institute (VJTI) for another (hackathon). Fun part is first. Tech part is below the fun part of this post. As always, during the month end, I face a scarcity of GitHub CoPilot credits, so I started coding on second day of the hackathon at 5:30 am (limits resetted at March 1st) 🫠 . What did I do until that?? Harsh , ujjjwal and me roamed and roamed around the campus, it's what you do at a hackathon. When we returned, we saw Sachin sir brainstorming with our peers. We were feeling pretty confident 💪 , thanks to four cans of Red Bull which the organizers graciously provided, and approached him with our ideas which we discussed till late night. This is where we met new friends Joel , Shashank , Dhruv and many (I don't have their Linkedins) others who were in for fun. Until 5:30, we talked, shared experiences (sage advice from my seniors) and laughed like mad 😁 . I was half sure if it wasn't for Bharat sir being chill, we would have been removed from the campus. Before pitching round, we all were test subjects of Joel and Shashank 's project. Theirs was a missile detection and citizens evacuation system, where for demo, the missile (a Red Bull can) was thrown at the guy shooting the video (me) 🤕 . It was a good project, but I still don't appreciate being hit. Now let's come to the tech part. M-Indicator AI hackathon was the name, and hence my pea sized brain decided to integrate AI to the already crazy M-Indicator app. I would have loved to share the source code, but I hope you would directly view it in your app. Well, the AI turned which I added were: 1. Custom RAPTOR algo on M-Indicator's transit data which suggests trains depending on user's behaviour with explainable AI. 2. Various gaussian, bayesian and regression models for calculating seating probability (whether your legs would hurt), friction commute (add shoulders hurting too) etc. and many more. 3. Meetup Scheduling with friends. I used (I can neither pronounce it or understand it yet) a Wasserstein-2 barycenter optimisation over Gaussian arrival distributions. I had a coarse logic which worked quite well but this equation worked better than anything.. so yeah I vibecoded this. Dhruv conveniently spotted my algos calculated the shortest travel time but not the fastest arrival time possible when pitching had already started. Needless to say, I had to start from scratch and was the third last person to pitch ( Harsh and Joel were the next 2, frankly because we were busy having fun). When the winners were announced, me, Dhruv , Joel and Shashank were overjoyed to find we were winners (40k didn't hurt). Frankly, everyone seated there had fantastic projects, we had a good fortune going Thank you Sachin sir, Amal sir and Mahesh sir for being awesome and considering me worthy 🙏 . If you ever see me at a hackathon, please network with me 😊 Devansh Raulo
post_text_html:
Excited to announce I lost the CSI-SPIT hackathon recently 🥳 , and within a week I was shamelessly at Veermata Jijabai Technological Institute (VJTI) for another (hackathon).

Fun part is first. Tech part is below the fun part of this post.

As always, during the month end, I face a scarcity of GitHub CoPilot credits, so I started coding on second day of the hackathon at 5:30 am (limits resetted at March 1st) 🫠 . What did I do until that?? Harsh, ujjjwal and me roamed and roamed around the campus, it's what you do at a hackathon. When we returned, we saw Sachin sir brainstorming with our peers. We were feeling pretty confident 💪 , thanks to four cans of Red Bull which the organizers graciously provided, and approached him with our ideas which we discussed till late night.

This is where we met new friends Joel, Shashank, Dhruv and many (I don't have their Linkedins) others who were in for fun. Until 5:30, we talked, shared experiences (sage advice from my seniors) and laughed like mad 😁 . I was half sure if it wasn't for Bharat sir being chill, we would have been removed from the campus.

Before pitching round, we all were test subjects of Joel and Shashank's project. Theirs was a missile detection and citizens evacuation system, where for demo, the missile (a Red Bull can) was thrown at the guy shooting the video (me) 🤕 . It was a good project, but I still don't appreciate being hit.

Now let's come to the tech part. M-Indicator AI hackathon was the name, and hence my pea sized brain decided to integrate AI to the already crazy M-Indicator app. I would have loved to share the source code, but I hope you would directly view it in your app.
Well, the AI turned which I added were:
1. Custom RAPTOR algo on M-Indicator's transit data which suggests trains depending on user's behaviour with explainable AI.
2. Various gaussian, bayesian and regression models for calculating seating probability (whether your legs would hurt), friction commute (add shoulders hurting too) etc. and many more.
3. Meetup Scheduling with friends. I used (I can neither pronounce it or understand it yet) a Wasserstein-2 barycenter optimisation over Gaussian arrival distributions. I had a coarse logic which worked quite well but this equation worked better than anything.. so yeah I vibecoded this.

Dhruv conveniently spotted my algos calculated the shortest travel time but not the fastest arrival time possible when pitching had already started. Needless to say, I had to start from scratch and was the third last person to pitch (Harsh and Joel were the next 2, frankly because we were busy having fun).

When the winners were announced, me, Dhruv, Joel and Shashank were overjoyed to find we were winners (40k didn't hurt). Frankly, everyone seated there had fantastic projects, we had a good fortune going
Thank you Sachin sir, Amal sir and Mahesh sir for being awesome and considering me worthy 🙏 .

If you ever see me at a hackathon, please network with me 😊
Devansh Raulo

One of the highlights for me was meeting Sachin Teke, the founder of m-Indicator. Interacting with him and understanding the vision behind such initiatives was extremely inspiring.

What made this hackathon even more special was the community. I met some incredibly talented participants and previous hackathon winners. Their passion, mindset, and attitude toward solving real-world problems were truly motivating. The networking, idea exchanges, and learning from each other were just as valuable as the competition itself.

Grateful for the opportunity to be part of such an energetic environment filled with builders, innovators, and problem solvers.

Looking forward to participating in many more such events and continuing the journey of building with AI. 🤖✨

Great interview touching all the right points. I have been following ControlAI for a while now and I suggest you do the same (if you care about the future)

Anthropic recently disclosed that three AI labs allegedly ran large-scale distillation attacks on Claude — using ~24,000 fraudulent accounts to generate over 16 million interactions aimed at replicating model capabilities. Distillation itself isn’t new. It’s a legitimate technique to compress or transfer knowledge between models. The controversy begins when it becomes a shortcut to copy capabilities instead of building them. A few reflections: • The real AI battleground may shift from training models to protecting model behavior • “Model moats” look thinner — APIs can become the new extraction surface • Expect tighter access controls, stricter verification, and more aggressive usage monitoring across providers • Developers should treat LLM access like critical infrastructure — have fallback providers and contingency plans The next phase of AI competition may not be about who trains the biggest model — but who can secure, govern, and operationalize intelligence most effectively. #ArtificialIntelligence #GenerativeAI #LLM #AIInfrastructure #AIGovernance #AIEthics #FutureOfAI

This trend is exposing the reality of our security culture, people are blindly feeding AI their faces, surroundings, habits, and pieces of their personal lives, with zero understanding of where that data goes, how long it’s stored, or who can access it. It’s exhausting trying to teach people how to safeguard their information while they chase trends, not realizing they’re handing over personal data to systems they don’t understand, with no knowledge of how it will be used. The danger isn’t AI, it’s human behavior. The weakest link in the digital world has always been ourselves.

title: Neptune.js - The Game Engine Born from a Challenge
headline: I've always been fascinated by the challenges of creating games within constraints as a game developer. That's why I was intrigued when I heard about the Floppy Disk Game Jam last year.
post_text: I've always been fascinated by the challenges of creating games within constraints as a game developer. That's why I was intrigued when I heard about the Floppy Disk Game Jam last year. The game jam compelled that the memory size of the game be just under 80kb, that seemed unimaginable at first. Instead of surrendering, I decided to take on the challenge and develop my own game engine that could work within this restrictions. That's how Neptune.js came to be. I started developing Neptune.js on April 5, 2022, and today, almost a year later, I am proud to say that the engine has come a long way. Neptune.js is a set of JavaScript modules specifically designed for writing video games and multimedia programs using the JavaScript language. It adds functionality on top of HTML5 canvas and is highly portable, running on browsers. What sets Neptune.js apart is its minimalist design. The core is simple, and the code is documented neatly, making it easy to use. Even kids can make a simple game in Neptune. The engine is modular, allowing developers to use specific pieces of it separately. Neptune.js is fast and handles the main loop and event cycles under the hood, providing developers with a clean yet customizable feature set. One of the most significant advantages of Neptune.js is its entity component-based system, making it more advanced than previous versions. This system is used by many major engines and provides numerous advantages. Although the learning curve is moderate, the system is worth mastering. During the Floppy Disk Game Jam, I used Neptune.js to create my game, and I am thrilled to say that my game, "Singularity Clicker: Birth of Infinity ," made with Neptune.js, won 3rd prize. The judges were impressed with the game engine, and it even sparked some interest among other developers in the game jam. As we approach the one-year mark of Neptune.js's development, I am excited to see what the future holds for this game engine. I am constantly working to improve Neptune.js and adding new features to make it an even more powerful tool for game developers. #gamejam #gamedevelopment #javascript #minimalistdesign #modular #opensource #videogames #multimedia #programming #tech #innovation

title: #hackathon #building #mentor | Mohit Nippanikar | 23 comments
headline: 𝐌𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐨𝐫.
post_text: 𝐌𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐨𝐫. 𝐓𝐡𝐚𝐭'𝐬 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐢𝐭𝐞 𝐬𝐚𝐢𝐝. I wasn't sure I earned it yet. Still learning. prepping for placements. Still building my startup. Still keeping up with the latest tech. I'm not done being a student. But Umar Khan called anyway. "w𝘦 𝘸𝘢𝘯𝘵 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘢𝘴 𝘢 𝘔𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘰𝘳." Same room. Same energy. Different chair. And that changed everything. I chose FinTech. My domain. My battleground for 2 years. Alongside Manav Shah , we sat across 11 teams. 5-10 minutes each. But before I entered that room? 𝐈 𝐝𝐢𝐝 𝐦𝐲 𝐡𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤. Read every problem statement like I was building it myself. Mapped existing competitors. Identified market gaps. Figured out what the best possible product could look like. Before they even pitched. Because that's what an ideal mentor owes every team. Not just opinions. Not just motivation. Real preparation. Then I learned the hardest part. 𝐋𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠. Some teams were brilliant but scattered. Some were confident but solving the wrong problem. Some were raw but asking questions that made me rethink my own approach. And here's what nobody tells you about mentoring.. Every team shared their idea with me in trust. Their MVP. Their pitch angle. Their secret edge. And I kept every single one. I didn't compare Team A to Team B. A mentor doesn't leak. A mentor protects the room they've been given. Guided them on.. What do the judges want to see? What does the user actually need? What does your idea solve that no one else can? Not my answers. Theirs refined through the right questions. That's the difference between giving direction and building clarity. Here's the truth about mentoring: Competing is about controlling your execution. Building your solution. Fixing your bugs. Winning your pitch. Mentoring? You control nothing. You guide. You suggest. You nudge. You hope they see what you're pointing at. Because somewhere between guiding teams.. I stopped being just a competitor. I became a builder of competitors. And honestly? 𝘛𝘩𝘢𝘵 𝘮𝘪𝘨𝘩𝘵 𝘣𝘦 𝘵𝘩𝘦 𝘣𝘪𝘨𝘨𝘦𝘴𝘵 𝘸𝘪𝘯 𝘰𝘧 𝘮𝘺 𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘪𝘳𝘦 𝘩𝘢𝘤𝘬𝘢𝘵𝘩𝘰𝘯 𝘫𝘰𝘶𝘳𝘯𝘦𝘺. Grateful to be back to Shri Bhagubhai Mafatlal Polytechnic !!! — Mohit Nippanikar | curious enough to break things across every field #Hackathon #Building #Mentor

𝐌𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐨𝐫. 𝐓𝐡𝐚𝐭'𝐬 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐢𝐧𝐯𝐢𝐭𝐞 𝐬𝐚𝐢𝐝. I wasn't sure I earned it yet.

Still learning. prepping for placements.
Still building my startup.
Still keeping up with the latest tech.

I'm not done being a student.

But Umar Khan called anyway.
"w𝘦 𝘸𝘢𝘯𝘵 𝘺𝘰𝘶 𝘢𝘴 𝘢 𝘔𝘦𝘯𝘵𝘰𝘳."

Same room. Same energy. Different chair.
And that changed everything.



I chose FinTech. My domain. My battleground for 2 years.

Alongside Manav Shah, we sat across 11 teams. 5-10 minutes each.



But before I entered that room? 𝐈 𝐝𝐢𝐝 𝐦𝐲 𝐡𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐰𝐨𝐫𝐤.

Read every problem statement like I was building it myself.
Mapped existing competitors.
Identified market gaps.
Figured out what the best possible product could look like.

Before they even pitched.
Because that's what an ideal mentor owes every team. Not just opinions. Not just motivation.
Real preparation.



Then I learned the hardest part. 𝐋𝐢𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠.

Some teams were brilliant but scattered.
Some were confident but solving the wrong problem.
Some were raw but asking questions that made me rethink my own approach.


And here's what nobody tells you about mentoring..

Every team shared their idea with me in trust.
Their MVP.
Their pitch angle.
Their secret edge.
And I kept every single one.

I didn't compare Team A to Team B.
A mentor doesn't leak.
A mentor protects the room they've been given.



Guided them on..
What do the judges want to see? What does the user actually need? What does your idea solve that no one else can?

Not my answers. Theirs refined through the right questions.
That's the difference between giving direction and building clarity.



Here's the truth about mentoring:
Competing is about controlling your execution.
Building your solution.
Fixing your bugs.
Winning your pitch.
Mentoring? You control nothing.

You guide. You suggest. You nudge.
You hope they see what you're pointing at.



Because somewhere between guiding teams..
I stopped being just a competitor.
I became a builder of competitors.
And honestly?

headline: We just won $120,000 at the Y Combinator Hackathon
post_text: We just won $120,000 at the Y Combinator Hackathon This now makes me the youngest person ever to place 1st overall at a YC hackathon (age: 18). Shoutout to my team Hrishikesh Athreya , Arnav D. , and Aditi Apoorva Jagalur Raghuvara (from SCU). We built Vital Link using OpenAI, Manufact, and Cloudflare, by integrating health data from different personal devices onto ChatGPT securely. Between us, we have a combined 55 hackathon wins (some overlapping), and we used all of that experience to execute fast under pressure. In the end, we walked away with: • $120,000 in prizes • a YC interview with a General Partner (upcoming batch) What people don’t see is that I’ve lost 9 YC hackathons before this and only won 2nd (track prize). This one means a lot. Huge shoutout to the sponsors for making this event incredible and the prize pool so big Manufact (formerly mcp-use) , OpenAI , Cloudflare , Puzzle , and of course the YC General Partner.

title: Building ConceptForge: Custom Real-Time Game Engine in C++ with OpenGL | Kshitij Aucharmal posted on the topic | LinkedIn
headline: Building my own game engine from scratch — and it's starting to feel alive.
post_text: Building my own game engine from scratch — and it's starting to feel alive. ConceptForge is a custom real-time engine I'm creating in C++ with OpenGL. It uses an ECS architecture (Entity-Component-System) — a flexible way to build game objects by mixing reusable pieces (like position, light, or model) instead of rigid classes. This makes everything faster and easier to extend. Plus, Python scripting is baked in, so eventually you can just describe what you want in plain English and have AI help generate it. This month I pushed hard: - Revived the in-engine editor with a live Python code runner (hit Run → see "Hello World" + time print instantly) - Added proper scene hierarchy (drag objects to parent them, move lights around a cathedral) - Implemented advanced graphics tricks: depth & stencil buffers for clean outlines, a cool toon shader for stylized looks, antialiasing, and full shadow mapping (with soft edges via PCF filtering — tested on a detailed backpack model) Videos attached show the progress: orbiting lights casting real shadows, smooth borders, hierarchy in action. 121 commits total. Still early days, but the foundation is solid and fun to build on. I post daily on my X account: https://x.com/ma_at_anubis With devlogs here: https://lnkd.in/eZFxzqWa Repo here if you're curious: https://lnkd.in/eYSNmcw9 Thoughts on ECS engines, shadows, or AI integration? Would love to hear them. #GameDev #GraphicsProgramming #IndieDev #Cpp #OpenGL #ConceptForge
post_text_html:
Building my own game engine from scratch — and it's starting to feel alive.

ConceptForge is a custom real-time engine I'm creating in C++ with OpenGL. It uses an ECS architecture (Entity-Component-System) — a flexible way to build game objects by mixing reusable pieces (like position, light, or model) instead of rigid classes. This makes everything faster and easier to extend.

Plus, Python scripting is baked in, so eventually you can just describe what you want in plain English and have AI help generate it.
This month I pushed hard:
- Revived the in-engine editor with a live Python code runner (hit Run → see "Hello World" + time print instantly)
- Added proper scene hierarchy (drag objects to parent them, move lights around a cathedral)
- Implemented advanced graphics tricks: depth & stencil buffers for clean outlines, a cool toon shader for stylized looks, antialiasing, and full shadow mapping (with soft edges via PCF filtering — tested on a detailed backpack model)

Videos attached show the progress: orbiting lights casting real shadows, smooth borders, hierarchy in action.

121 commits total. Still early days, but the foundation is solid and fun to build on.

I post daily on my X account: https://x.com/ma_at_anubis
With devlogs here: https://lnkd.in/eZFxzqWa

Repo here if you're curious: https://lnkd.in/eYSNmcw9

Thoughts on ECS engines, shadows, or AI integration?
Would love to hear them.

Big step forward for ConceptForge! After hitting a wall with nanobind (it simply doesn’t support embedding Python into C++ the way I needed), I made the switch to pybind11. And it works beautifully. Just wired up a quick add_cube() binding for testing, and here’s the result: a smooth cube spawning and rotating right inside the engine. Nothing fancy yet… but it’s the foundation for something much bigger. Being able to embed full Python scripting directly into the C++ core opens up insane possibilities: - Rapid prototyping of gameplay mechanics - AI-driven tools & behaviors - Hot-reloading logic without recompiling - And eventually letting creators script their own games in Python while the heavy graphics & performance stay in C++ This is exactly the kind of hybrid power I’ve been chasing. ConceptForge is starting to feel real now. Can’t wait to push this further — next up: exposing more engine systems to Python and building actual logic with it. What do you think — would you rather script your game engine in Python or stick to pure C++? Drop your thoughts below 👇 #ConceptForge #GameDev #GraphicsProgramming #Python #C ++ #GameEngine #IndieDev #AI

Hi Guys!!! 😄 I spent the las few weeks experimenting with mathematical and matrix operations inside Maya and put together a small tool that simulates a force field. The script: • Generates a spherical force field, and • Instantiates multiple meshes across a chosen geometry, placing each instance at the geometry’s vertex world positions. Under the hood I leaned on Maya nodes for distance/vector math; the calculations use the field’s world position, its radius, and each mesh’s world-space center. The meshes themselves can be any shapes the user prefers, so it’s pretty flexible. At the same time, each generated mesh has its own controls, so you can animate each one individually by hand. Or, using the UI you can choose a start and end frame and the values to animate the rotation and/or scale for each instance. I’d appreciate any feedback or ideas for new features. Happy to share this short demo! #3d #TechnicalArt #Maya #Python #MayaPython #Scripting #ToolDevelopment #3DTools #Pipeline #TechnicalArtist #Rigging #Animation #VFX #3DArt #PluginsMaya #DigitalArt #Workflow #StudioTools #QTDesginer

I’ve spent this morning getting a simple MCP bridge for Houdini sorted on my local machine. I like seeing these sort of use cases for language models in VFX, though I reckon there’s still a healthy gap between a flashy demo and a production ready workflow. The advantage at the moment is definitely VEX and Python automation. Having an LLM that’s actually context-aware of the live session is a massive help for the logic side of things. It's a genuine workflow boost for generating snippets on the fly. That said, you do still need to keep a close eye on it when it comes to actual scene architecture. Like in the video below, I was testing a simple particle setup and Claude decided to get a bit over-excited, it wired in a redunant collision node and overcomplicated a simple rule setup that could be done with one wrangle. It’s brilliant at the code, but it’s still prone to over egging the pudding on the layout side. Very handy tool to have in the arsenal for scripting and prototyping, provided you’re there to tidy up after it. I have seen some plugins and tools like an AI Assistant for Houdini with keys to use many other LLMs, would love to hear from people who have used it and if there are any benefits to it compared to connecting specific language models with an MCP #Houdini #SideFX #TechnicalArt #VFX #ClaudeAI #MCP #Pipeline #VEX

Visualising Scala: The Mechanics of a .map 🧬 Following up on my S3 Glacier animation, I wanted to apply the same visual logic to a core functional programming concept: the Scala map function. In Scala, map is more than just a loop; it’s a transformation that yields a brand-new collection while leaving the original data untouched. What’s happening in this Rive animation? 📥 The Input: We start with an immutable collection of integers. ⚙️ The Transformation: Each element passes through the transformer function f(x) = 10 * x. 📤 The Result: A new collection is produced with the transformed values. Building these visualisations is helping me bridge the gap between high-level system architecture and low-level code logic. #Scala #FunctionalProgramming #SoftwareEngineering #RiveApp #DataVisualization #LearningInPublic #JVM #Coding

My team and I built a Sorting Algorithm Visualizer using Python and Pygame for my CS335 course — and it turned out to be one of the more rewarding projects I've worked on. The idea is straightforward: instead of just reading about how sorting algorithms work, you can actually watch them in action. The app renders arrays as color-coded bars and animates each comparison, swap, and merge in real time, making it easy to see the differences between algorithms. What's included: 5 sorting algorithms: Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort, Merge Sort, Quick Sort, and Radix Sort Linear Search visualization Color-coded animation — green for the current position, red for comparisons and swaps Ascending and descending sort toggle with adjustable array sizes from 5 to 100 elements UI with custom-built interactive components On the technical side: Used Python's generator pattern to pause algorithms mid-execution, enabling frame-by-frame animation without multithreading Kept the architecture modular — configuration, algorithms, UI, and rendering each live in their own dedicated modules Pygame handles all the rendering at a consistent 60 FPS Working through this gave me a stronger grasp of algorithm complexity and reinforced solid habits around code organization and separation of concerns. Watching Radix Sort do its thing never gets old. #CSUF #AlgorithmEngineering #Spring2026

In building Animus ( https://lnkd.in/eHjymfzg ) I discovered something (that might have been common sense to some people). I set out to create a locally running, agentic CLI Tool, much like #Codex or #ClaudeCode , and I succeeded! … the only problem is the “fully local” part still doesn’t work (for the vast majority of people) and that’s due to the physical constraints of computation itself. To run a model that can do any kind of coherent actions at all within the terminal, you need around a 30b model with proper quantization. Unfortunately, at this level, it’s still not a reliably usable tool. This is around what most upper level consumer and gaming hardware can do. Around 70b to 100b is when multi step coding and file calling functions *start* to become possible. This is like enthusiast gaming/light engineering workstation hardware. Only at around 200b (and with a lot of RAM), or a model only accessible on heavy engineering workstation/ Data Center grade computation power, do you get something that will execute complex commands, have a large enough context window to be able to consider “compressed history” files, and actually feel like it’s doing what you say. And even then, API access to Opus 4.5 or Codex 5.2 wins, both in performance and amortized cost over the life of the computer. And that’s why Animus ended up with an API harness 😂
